Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd (in Liquidation)
Party_Details.Facts: AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd is a company incorporated in Singapore with objects of trading in metals and providing financial services. From about October 2007, its directors included Mr Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan, Mr Ong Kee Ming Richard, and Mr Koh Ting Giap. For many years AMG did little business but incurred expenses including rental and renovation costs. In 2017, AMG entered into a discounting/monetizing arrangement and memorandum of understanding dated 21 June 2017 with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd (F&BCo), under which AMG was to receive an irrevocable confirmed documentary letter of credit (DLC) for US$2,860,000 issued by Kasikornbank Public Company Limited of Bangkok, Thailand, in respect of a purported sale of 1,000 metric tons of aluminium sheets. Under the MOU, AMG was to pay 75% of the DLC value (US$2,145,000) to F&BCo within three business days and 5% (US$143,000) to White Lotus Cosmetics Limited as a service fee, retaining 20% (US$572,000) as a non‑recourse payment. On 25 August 2017, US$2,764,104.53 was deposited into AMG’s account with Standard Chartered Bank (Singapore) Limited (the SCB Account), which previously had a zero balance apart from a small deposit of US$7.22 on 23 August 2017. AMG did not supply F&BCo with the 1,000 metric tons of aluminium sheets and did not pay the agreed US$2,145,000 to F&BCo. F&BCo issued a statutory demand dated 22 November 2017 for US$2,145,000. AMG appears to have had no assets other than the balance in the SCB Account and no other business generating revenue. Once the SCB Account balance fell below US$2,145,000 on or about 29 August 2017, AMG could no longer pay that debt. AMG was wound up on 9 March 2018 on the application of F&BCo based on the unsatisfied statutory demand. The liquidator, Mr Abuthahir s/o Abdul Gafoor of AAG Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd, brought this action against two former directors in relation to withdrawals and payments from the SCB Account and a facility obtained from SCB secured over a fixed deposit.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant directors breached their duties and incurred liability, including potential accessory liability, in relation to withdrawals and payments from the company’s bank account and the use of a banking facility obtained by the company.
Party_Details.Rule: The judgment discusses directors’ duties at general law and under s 157 of the Companies Act (Cap 50, 2006 Rev Ed), including the duties to act bona fide in the interests of the company, to act for proper corporate purposes, to avoid conflicts between personal interest and duty, and to exercise reasonable skill and care. It refers to the requirement under s 169 of the Companies Act that directors’ remuneration be approved by a resolution not related to other matters, and to s 145(5) of the Companies Act, which prevents a director from resigning if no other director ordinarily resident in Singapore remains. It also considers s 391 of the Companies Act, which empowers the court to relieve a person from liability for negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust if he has acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused. The judgment sets out the elements of knowing receipt as articulated in George Raymond Zage III and another v Ho Chi Kwong and another [2010] 2 SLR 589, and refers to principles on directors’ duties to consider creditors’ interests when a company is approaching insolvency, including Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and others [2004] 1 SLR(R) 434. It also mentions the principle that unanimous informal shareholder assent can in some circumstances validate corporate acts, as in Re Duomatic Ltd [1969] 2 Ch 365 and Yong Kheng Leong and another v Panweld Trading Pte Ltd and another [2013] 1 SLR 173.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined the factual background of AMG’s lack of prior business activity, the sudden inflow of DLC proceeds, and the structure of the MOU with F&BCo and White Lotus. It analysed the sequence of withdrawals and payments from the SCB Account, distinguishing between early transactions where reliance on explanations by the director who procured the DLC could be viewed in context of his apparent success, and later transactions where the size, frequency, and lack of documentation or plausible commercial purpose indicated that the funds were being removed without benefit to AMG. The court considered the roles and knowledge of each director, including their understanding of AMG’s financial position, the absence of internal controls or staff‑based checking systems, and the fact that AMG’s only asset was the SCB Account balance. It evaluated whether each director took reasonable steps to understand the purpose of payments, sought supporting documentation, and maintained an enquiring mind, or instead acquiesced in unexplained withdrawals. The court assessed the mental health evidence relating to one director to determine whether his condition affected his understanding of the transactions or his capacity to discharge his duties, and considered his obtaining of a handwritten confirmation from the other director assuming responsibility for payments as evidence of his awareness of potential liability. It reviewed evidence of historical capital and loan contributions by another director, including emails predating the dispute that recorded cash, loan, and expense amounts, to determine whether he reasonably believed he was owed money by AMG and how that belief influenced his conduct when he re‑joined the board. The court analysed the legal character of payments described as directors’ fees or loan repayments in light of statutory requirements for approval of remuneration, the absence of shareholder resolutions, the lack of clear bases for the amounts, and the timing relative to AMG’s insolvency. It examined the circumstances under which one director agreed to become the locally resident director while authorising the other director as sole bank signatory, including his knowledge that the outgoing director had been reluctant to sign cheques, to determine whether he deliberately refrained from acting as a check on the other director. For the knowing receipt analysis, the court considered whether payments received by a director before he re‑joined the board were traceable to misapplication of AMG’s assets and whether he knew that the funds were being transferred in breach of fiduciary duty, having regard to his own description of the conditions on which he agreed to return and the context of the other director’s conduct. In relation to the SCB facility secured over a fixed deposit, the court differentiated between the initial establishment of the facility, which involved a commercial banking arrangement, and a later drawdown supported by documents that did not correspond to any genuine underlying transaction, assessing whether losses from that drawdown and associated interest were connected to the directors’ conduct. Finally, the court considered whether either director satisfied the statutory criteria for relief under s 391 of the Companies Act by acting honestly and reasonably in the circumstances of their appointments and the company’s financial position.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court held the defendants liable in specified monetary sums with interest and declined to grant relief under s 391 of the Companies Act.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ong Kee Ming Richard
Party_Details.Facts: Mr Ong Kee Ming Richard is a Singapore citizen who was a director of AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd from 13 September 2006 to 25 September 2017. He was a joint signatory, together with Mr Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan, to AMG’s account with Standard Chartered Bank (Singapore) Limited during the period 23 August 2017 to 21 September 2017. During this period, substantial funds from a documentary letter of credit arrangement with Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd were deposited into the SCB Account, and a series of withdrawals and payments were made. Mr Ong signed off on nineteen withdrawals and payments from the SCB Account and also signed a security agreement in relation to a facility granted by Standard Chartered Bank to AMG secured over a fixed deposit of US$1,000,000. He had been a director of AMG for many years during which the company did little business. He received a payment of US$74,699.11 from AMG on 28 August 2017, which was described as remuneration for his services as a director. He later resigned as director on 25 September 2017. In September 2017 he attended Clementi Polyclinic and was referred to the National University Hospital for investigation of a possible psychosis episode, and he was later seen by a psychiatric team with an impression of psychotic depression. He was warded at the Institute of Mental Health for about three weeks from 15 October 2017. On or about 11 August 2017 he obtained a handwritten confirmation from Mr Manu stating that Mr Manu took full responsibility for payments made from the SCB Account and for the procurement and supply of goods mentioned in the DLC.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether Mr Ong, as a director and joint bank signatory, breached his duties and incurred liability, including potential knowing receipt, in relation to specific withdrawals and payments from AMG’s bank account and the execution of a banking facility, and whether any mental health issues or statutory relief could affect his liability.
Party_Details.Rule: The court applied general law principles on directors’ fiduciary duties and duties of care and skill, as reflected in s 157 of the Companies Act, including the duties to act bona fide in the interests of the company, to act for proper corporate purposes, to avoid conflicts between personal interest and duty, and to exercise reasonable skill and care. It considered that a director authorising payments must believe they are for proper corporate purposes and must take reasonable steps to understand the transactions and review supporting documentation, while being entitled to rely honestly and reasonably on information from fellow directors and existing internal checks. The judgment referred to s 169 of the Companies Act, which requires that directors’ remuneration be approved by a resolution not related to other matters, and to the possibility of informal unanimous shareholder assent under the Duomatic principle as discussed in Re Duomatic Ltd and Yong Kheng Leong v Panweld Trading Pte Ltd. It discussed s 145(5) of the Companies Act, which prevents a director from resigning if no other director ordinarily resident in Singapore remains, and s 391 of the Companies Act, which allows the court to relieve a person from liability for negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust if he has acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused. For knowing receipt, the court adopted the elements set out in George Raymond Zage III and another v Ho Chi Kwong and another, namely a disposal of assets in breach of fiduciary duty, beneficial receipt by the defendant, and knowledge making it unconscionable to retain the benefit.
Party_Details.Application: The court reviewed each transaction that Mr Ong countersigned, considering the timing, amount, stated purpose, and whether any supporting documents were seen by him. For early payments and withdrawals shortly after the DLC proceeds were received, such as a cash withdrawal for the other director’s personal expenses, repayment to a trading counterparty, and payments for purported travel expenses, the court evaluated whether Mr Ong could reasonably rely on the explanations given in light of the apparent success of the DLC transaction and AMG’s stated business of trading in metals. As the sequence progressed to larger and less explained withdrawals, including substantial cash withdrawals and payments to individuals and entities with no clear connection to AMG’s business, the court examined Mr Ong’s own testimony that he objected to certain transactions, expressed a desire to resign, and recognised that the amounts involved were very large. It considered his admission that he did not obtain explanations or documentation for some payments and that he understood that some withdrawals did not benefit AMG. The court assessed the point at which Mr Ong’s reliance on the other director’s assurances ceased to be reasonable, focusing on a large payment to an individual that Mr Ong said marked a turning point in his state of mind. It analysed the payment of US$74,699.11 to Mr Ong as purported director’s remuneration against the statutory requirement for shareholder approval, the absence of any basis for the amount or period covered, and the company’s financial position approaching insolvency, and considered whether the payment was in substance an attempt to secure his cooperation. The court evaluated Mr Ong’s mental health evidence, including the clinic memo and subsequent hospitalisation, to determine whether he understood his actions at the time of the transactions, noting his ability to obtain a written confirmation from the other director assuming responsibility for payments as evidence of his awareness of potential liability. It considered his argument that he felt constrained by s 145(5) of the Companies Act from resigning as the only locally resident director, and analysed whether this affected his duty to refuse to authorise payments he believed were improper. In relation to the SCB facility, the court distinguished between the establishment of the facility, which involved a commercial banking arrangement that could be seen as beneficial for trading, and later misuse of the facility after Mr Ong had left the board. The court also considered whether Mr Ong’s receipt of funds from AMG could satisfy the elements of knowing receipt, examining whether the payment was traceable to a breach of fiduciary duty and whether he knew that it was improper. Finally, the court assessed whether Mr Ong’s conduct met the statutory criteria for relief under s 391, focusing on whether his actions, though taken under pressure, could be regarded as reasonable in light of his knowledge of the other director’s conduct and AMG’s circumstances.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court found that Mr Ong was liable for breaches of duty in relation to specified withdrawals and payments and for an improper payment to himself, and it awarded a quantified monetary sum with interest against him while declining to relieve him from liability under s 391 of the Companies Act.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Koh Ting Giap
Party_Details.Facts: Mr Koh Ting Giap is a Singapore citizen who was a director of AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd from 13 September 2006 to 10 January 2014, and again from 25 September 2017 until AMG was wound up. In the early years of AMG, he was involved in providing, through Weeds Natural Remedies Pte Ltd, AMG’s original business involving health supplement products, including formulae and an eight‑year licence to use certain registered trade marks, and he held shares in AMG, many of which were held as nominee for Weeds. He later left AMG around 2014 because AMG had not brought in business or funds. Before leaving, he had contributed cash, loans, and expenses for office rental and renovation, which he recorded in emails in 2009 to Mr Manu and to Mr Ong, stating total amounts in the hundreds of thousands of dollars. He testified that he believed AMG owed him a substantial sum for these contributions. When he re‑joined AMG in 2017 at the request of Mr Manu, he did so as the required locally resident director and did not hold shares in AMG, as by then Mr Manu had become the 100% owner. At that time, only Mr Manu was the signatory of AMG’s bank account with Standard Chartered Bank. Shortly after re‑joining, Mr Koh and Mr Manu signed a directors’ resolution dated 26 September 2017 authorising Mr Manu to be the sole signatory for all banking facilities with Standard Chartered Bank. After Mr Koh’s return, a series of withdrawals and payments were made from the SCB Account, including cash withdrawals, payments to various individuals and entities, and payments to Mr Koh himself. A payment of US$37,458.77 was made to Mr Koh on 21 September 2017 before he formally re‑joined as a director, and a further payment of US$37,247.43 was made to him on 29 September 2017 after he became a director. He described these payments as partial repayment of his loans to AMG and as part of an arrangement under which he agreed to re‑join the board. During his second tenure as director, a drawdown of US$744,000 was made under a facility from Standard Chartered Bank secured over a fixed deposit, supported by an invoice and delivery order from Smart Recycling Pte Ltd, although there was no evidence of a genuine underlying transaction between AMG and Smart Recycling.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether Mr Koh, as a director during the relevant period and as a recipient of company funds, breached his duties and incurred liability, including for knowing receipt and for losses arising from a banking facility drawdown, and whether his belief that he was owed loans by the company or any statutory relief affected his liability.
Party_Details.Rule: The court applied general fiduciary and care and skill duties of directors under common law and s 157 of the Companies Act, including the duties to act bona fide in the interests of the company, to act for proper corporate purposes, to avoid conflicts between personal interest and duty, and to exercise reasonable skill and care. It considered that a director must safeguard the company’s assets and cannot abdicate oversight by giving another director unchecked control over bank accounts, especially where there are indications of improper conduct. The judgment referred to the principle that when a company is approaching insolvency, directors must consider the interests of creditors, as discussed in Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and others. It also applied the elements of knowing receipt from George Raymond Zage III and another v Ho Chi Kwong and another, requiring a disposal of assets in breach of fiduciary duty, beneficial receipt by the defendant, and knowledge making it unconscionable to retain the benefit. The court considered s 391 of the Companies Act, which allows relief from liability where a person has acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused, and noted that the expiry of a limitation period does not extinguish an underlying debt but only bars a remedy. It recognised that a director may bargain for repayment of loans as a condition of joining a board, subject to disclosure and recusal, but may not agree to refrain from oversight in return for such repayment.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined Mr Koh’s historical involvement with AMG, including his role in providing the original health supplement business through Weeds and his emails from 2009 recording cash, loan, and expense contributions, to determine whether he genuinely believed AMG owed him substantial sums. It accepted that he had made loans and incurred expenses on AMG’s behalf, though the exact amount could not be quantified from the available evidence, and noted that any limitation issues would not extinguish the underlying debt. The court then analysed the circumstances of his return to AMG in 2017, focusing on his own evidence that he re‑joined partly to recover his loans and that he agreed to terms under which Mr Manu would be the 100% shareholder, CEO, and sole bank signatory. It considered his admission that he took the opportunity to seek repayment of his loans when invited back and his understanding that the other director complained that the outgoing director had made signing cheques difficult. The court found that this information put Mr Koh on notice that the outgoing director had disagreed with some payments or withdrawals, and it evaluated his failure to speak to the outgoing director about these disagreements as a decision to avoid learning details that might have required him to act. In assessing the directors’ resolution making the other director the sole signatory, the court considered whether Mr Koh’s explanation that this would allow faster business development was credible in light of his knowledge of prior disagreements and AMG’s financial position, and whether he thereby chose not to act as a check on the other director. The court reviewed the series of cash withdrawals and payments made after Mr Koh became a director, noting the absence of documentation or clear commercial purpose for many of them, and considered whether these were consistent with legitimate business or with depletion of AMG’s only asset. It evaluated the payments made to Mr Koh himself, including the pre‑appointment payment of US$37,458.77 and the post‑appointment payment of US$37,247.43, against his own description that he conditioned his return on repayment of part of his loans, and analysed whether these payments were linked to an arrangement under which he would not scrutinise the other director’s conduct. For the knowing receipt claim in respect of the pre‑appointment payment, the court considered whether the payment was made in breach of the other director’s fiduciary duty to AMG and whether Mr Koh knew that he was receiving funds as part of a scheme to strip AMG of assets. Regarding the SCB facility drawdown of US$744,000 to Smart Recycling Pte Ltd, the court examined the invoice and delivery order presented to the bank and the absence of any genuine underlying transaction, and assessed whether the loss from this drawdown and the associated interest were causally connected to Mr Koh’s decision to authorise the other director as sole signatory and to refrain from oversight. Finally, the court considered whether Mr Koh’s conduct satisfied the criteria for relief under s 391 of the Companies Act, focusing on whether he acted honestly and reasonably in light of his knowledge of AMG’s insolvency, his personal interest in loan repayment, and his decision to give the other director a free rein over the company’s only asset.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court found that Mr Koh was liable for breaches of duty in relation to withdrawals and payments made while he was a director, for knowing receipt of a payment made before he re‑joined the board, and for losses arising from an illegitimate drawdown under the banking facility, and it awarded a quantified monetary sum with interest against him while declining to relieve him from liability under s 391 of the Companies Act.
